petitioner_background: The petitioner is a classical double bassist seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA as an alien of extraordinary ability. He studied at the Julliard School (bachelor's and master's), performed as an ensemble member with numerous orchestras including the New England Symphonic Ensemble, Lyric Orchestra, Louisiana State University Orchestra, and Acadiana Symphony Orchestra, and participated in the Schleswig-Holstein Music Festival (1996 and 2000) and the Pacific Music Festival (1998). The petition was filed on March 10, 2005.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters of support from experts (including an Assistant Professor at Michigan State University School of Music) asserting that very few awards are available to classical double bassists because they play as part of ensembles rather than as soloists. Counsel argued that the petitioner's acceptance into the Julliard School, performances at distinguished venues like Carnegie Hall as part of an orchestral ensemble, and participation in international music festivals (Pacific Music Festival and Schleswig-Holstein Music Festival) were 'equivalent to the garnering of an individual award.' The petitioner submitted two certificates from the Director of the Orchestral Academy confirming his acceptance to the Schleswig-Holstein Music Festival Orchestral Academy in 1996 and 2000 and his participation in instructional 'chamber music workshops.' He also submitted an event program confirming selection as a 1998 Pacific Music Festival Academy participant, where he was among 114 individuals accepted to the 'Orchestra Course.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the letters of support did not establish that nationally or internationally recognized awards are nonexistent for classical musicians. The AAO rejected counsel's comparable evidence argument under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), stating that comparable evidence is only allowed if the ten criteria 'do not readily apply to the beneficiary's occupation,' and there was no indication the ten criteria could not establish eligibility in the petitioner's occupation. Regarding the music festivals, the AAO noted that both festivals were limited to young musicians — Schleswig-Holstein has an age limit of 26 and Pacific Music Festival was for musicians 'aged from 18 to 29.' The AAO found that participation in such training programs limited to young musicians in the early stages of their career does not constitute receipt of nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards. These festivals offered no meaningful comparison between the petitioner and musicians beyond their late twenties who had already completed advanced training. The AAO stated the classification is for individuals already at the top of their field, not those progressing toward the top, and these academies are intended for amateur musicians seeking training rather than seasoned professionals.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his membership in the International Society of Bassists. On appeal, counsel also argued that the petitioner's acceptance into the Julliard School met this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record did not include the membership bylaws or official admission requirements for the International Society of Bassists. There was no evidence that admission required outstanding achievement or that the petitioner was evaluated by national or international experts in consideration of his admission. Regarding the Julliard School argument, the AAO held that Julliard is an institution of higher learning rather than an association as contemplated by the regulations, and enrollment in bachelor's and master's studies is training for a future career, not membership in an association in the field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted two brief articles appearing in the New Jinshan Post on June 14, 2000 and August 14, 2004, discussing his performances at the Jinshan Theater in Shanghai, China. He also submitted a letter from an individual identifying himself as Chief Editor of a television program entitled 'Touring Around the Artistic World,' which appears on a 'satellite channel' of the 'Tianjin TV network,' stating that the program profiled the petitioner on October 2, 2004.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record included no evidence showing that the New Jinshan Post has substantial national readership. Regarding the television program, the AAO noted that the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii) requires submission of 'published materials about the alien ... in major media,' rather than third-party letters attesting to the existence of such material. The letter could not carry the same weight as the actual material (such as a complete transcript or video recording). There was no evidence showing how much time the program devoted to the petitioner or the program's viewership volume. The name 'Tianjin TV' suggested the program was shown locally rather than nationally.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted event programs from numerous performances in which he participated as an ensemble member, including frequent performances at Carnegie Hall as a member of the New England Symphonic Ensemble and performances at Alice Tully Hall located within the Julliard School building at Lincoln Center.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that this criterion is more appropriate for visual artists (such as sculptors and painters) rather than for classical musicians like the petitioner. In the performing arts, acclaim is generally not established by the mere act of appearing in public, but rather by attracting a substantial audience. The regulations establish separate criteria for those in the performing arts, and the petitioner's musical performances are far more relevant to the 'commercial successes in the performing arts' criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that he performed as a double bassist ensemble member for orchestras including the Lyric Orchestra, New England Symphonic Ensemble, Cosmopolitan Symphony Orchestra, Southeastern Louisiana University Chamber Orchestra, Rapides Symphony Orchestra, Louisiana State University Orchestra, Baton Rouge Symphony, Camerata of Los Angeles, Jupiter Symphony Chamber Players, and Acadiana Symphony Orchestra. He also submitted evidence that he served as principal bassist for the Louisiana State University Orchestra and the New England Symphonic Ensemble.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Aside from self-serving promotional material, there was no evidence showing that these orchestras had earned distinguished national reputations in the same manner as the New York Philharmonic, Boston Symphony Orchestra, or Chicago Symphony Orchestra. Regarding the Lyric Orchestra, an event program identified a different individual (not the petitioner) as the 'Principal' bassist. While the petitioner showed he served as principal bassist for LSU Orchestra and New England Symphonic Ensemble, these orchestras had not been shown to have distinguished national reputations. The petitioner also did not submit evidence showing he was the principal bassist in his current position with the Acadiana Symphony Orchestra.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a letter from the Director of MidAmerica Productions stating that the New England Symphonic Ensemble most recently performed at Carnegie Hall twice at the Isaac Stern Auditorium on Sunday June 12, 2005. The 2:00 PM show sold 1,600 tickets and the 8:00 PM show sold 1,200 tickets. The director stated the petitioner was 'essential to these pre-eminent, high-grossing performances.' The petitioner also submitted information from Carnegie Hall's website stating that the Isaac Stern Auditorium accommodates up to 2,804 people.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO calculated that the 2:00 p.m. show was only at 57 percent capacity and the 8:00 p.m. show was far below half capacity. The record included no evidence of documented 'sales' or 'receipts' showing that the petitioner's performances drew record crowds, were regular sell-out performances, or resulted in greater audiences than other similar performances that did not feature the petitioner. There was no evidence that the shows were promoted with the petitioner's name receiving top billing or that the popularity increased when the petitioner was known to be performing. Additionally, the performances described by the MidAmerica Productions director came into existence subsequent to the petition's filing date, and a petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing.

outcome_reasoning: The petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major internationally recognized award or that he met at least three of the regulatory criteria necessary to establish sustained acclaim as an alien of extraordinary ability. The AAO found that letters of support attesting to skill and the prestige of venues cannot serve as primary evidence for the regulatory criteria, and witness statements to the effect that the petitioner is widely acclaimed have minimal evidentiary value without objective evidence from independent sources. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself to such an extent that he had achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner, who did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted a procedural issue regarding the commercial success evidence: the Carnegie Hall performances described in the MidAmerica Productions letter (June 12, 2005) came into existence subsequent to the petition's filing date of March 10, 2005. Under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak, a petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing, so post-filing evidence could not be considered.
